stem: Wilkie v. Robbins declined to extend Bivens to a rancher's harassment claims against BLM officers. A central reason was:
opt_a: Property-rights disputes are governed exclusively by state law
opt_b: The rancher had not exhausted administrative remedies
opt_c: The unworkability of an unconstitutional-retaliation Bivens action without a clear standard
opt_d: Federal officers lack capacity to harass under federal law
explanation: Wilkie identified 'special factors' including administrability — a freestanding retaliation claim required courts to police any federal-officer conduct, an unworkable standard.

stem: Hans v. Louisiana extended state sovereign immunity to suits by:
opt_a: A state's own citizens raising federal claims, despite the 11th Amendment's text addressing only out-of-state plaintiffs
opt_b: Out-of-state citizens raising federal claims, consistent with 11A text
opt_c: Foreign sovereigns raising any federal claim
opt_d: Federal officers in their official capacity
explanation: Hans read 11A's penumbra to bar even in-state citizen federal claims, reasoning from the original presumption of state sovereign immunity.

stem: A plaintiff sues a state's attorney general in federal court seeking an injunction against enforcement of a state statute alleged to violate the U.S. Constitution. Under Ex parte Young, this is:
opt_a: Permitted only if Congress has expressly authorized the suit
opt_b: Permitted only if the state has waived sovereign immunity
opt_c: Barred by the 11th Amendment because the state is the real party in interest
opt_d: Permitted because the suit seeks prospective relief against an officer alleged to be acting unconstitutionally
explanation: Ex parte Young's officer-suit fiction permits prospective injunctive relief; an officer enforcing an unconstitutional law is stripped of state immunity for that purpose.

stem: Edelman v. Jordan distinguishes Ex parte Young from suits seeking:
opt_a: Declaratory judgment about the state's federal-law obligations
opt_b: Prospective compliance with federal law going forward
opt_c: Injunctive relief against state officers in their personal capacities
opt_d: Retroactive money damages payable from the state treasury
explanation: Edelman: Young permits prospective relief but bars retroactive damages from state treasury, even when styled as compliance.

stem: Pennhurst v. Halderman held that Ex parte Young does NOT permit:
opt_a: Federal-court injunctive enforcement of state law against state officers
opt_b: Federal-question suits against state officers
opt_c: Damages claims against state officers in personal capacity
opt_d: Class-action habeas claims
explanation: Pennhurst: federal courts cannot use Young to enforce state law against state officials — that would intrude on state sovereignty without supremacy-clause warrant.

stem: After Seminole Tribe v. Florida, Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity:
opt_a: Whenever it makes a clear statement
opt_b: Only under § 5 of the 14th Amendment, not under Article I commerce powers
opt_c: Under any enumerated power so long as state interests are not unduly burdened
opt_d: Only with consent of the Conference of Chief Justices
explanation: Seminole Tribe held that Article I powers cannot abrogate SSI; § 5 of the 14th Amendment can (Fitzpatrick).

stem: Central Virginia Community College v. Katz upheld bankruptcy proceedings against state interests because:
opt_a: Congress validly abrogated SSI under § 5 of the 14th Amendment
opt_b: States consented to federal bankruptcy jurisdiction in the plan of the constitutional convention
opt_c: The 11th Amendment does not address Article I bankruptcy
opt_d: Bankruptcy is a public-rights matter outside state immunity
explanation: Katz reasoned that ratification of the Bankruptcy Clause implicitly waived state immunity for bankruptcy proceedings — the 'plan of convention' theory.

stem: Torres v. Texas Department of Public Safety extended the plan-of-convention reasoning to:
opt_a: Suits enforcing the Spending Clause as applied to states
opt_b: Suits enforcing the Equal Protection Clause directly
opt_c: Suits enforcing federal employment protections under congressional war powers
opt_d: Suits enforcing federal admiralty law against state actors
explanation: Torres held that states yielded sovereign immunity for war-power-based federal claims (USERRA) under the plan of convention.

stem: Monroe v. Pape held that § 1983 reaches:
opt_a: Only state officials acting within the scope of their authority
opt_b: Federal officials acting jointly with state officials
opt_c: State officials acting under color of state law, even when violating state law
opt_d: Only state officials in their official capacity
explanation: Monroe interpreted 'under color of' broadly to reach abuses of authority — the heart of § 1983 enforcement against rogue officers.

stem: Monell v. NYC Department of Social Services held that a municipality is liable under § 1983:
opt_a: Whenever an employee acts under color of municipal authority
opt_b: Only in suits seeking injunctive relief
opt_c: Only when the deprivation arises from a policy or custom — not under respondeat superior
opt_d: Whenever the municipality fails to indemnify the offending employee
explanation: Monell's foundational rule: municipalities are 'persons' under § 1983 but liable only for policy/custom-based violations.

stem: Will v. Michigan State Police held that:
opt_a: State officials are categorically immune from § 1983 personal-capacity suits
opt_b: § 1983 does not reach state-law claims
opt_c: States and state agencies are not 'persons' under § 1983
opt_d: State courts lack jurisdiction over § 1983 claims
explanation: Will applied a clear-statement rule and concluded states/agencies aren't 'persons' for § 1983 damages, sidestepping 11A in the suit's framing.

stem: Hafer v. Melo distinguished Will by holding that state officers sued in their personal capacity:
opt_a: Are immune unless they exceeded their authority under state law
opt_b: Are entitled to compensation from state treasury upon judgment
opt_c: Are 'persons' under § 1983 even when their challenged acts were taken in office
opt_d: May raise the state's sovereign immunity defense
explanation: Hafer made personal-capacity suits viable: the official is the 'person' regardless of when conduct occurred, and the state's SSI does not transfer.

stem: Health & Hospital Corp. v. Talevski (2023) held that:
opt_a: Talevski overruled Gonzaga
opt_b: Spending-Clause statutes can create rights enforceable under § 1983 when the text uses rights-creating language
opt_c: Spending-Clause statutes never create § 1983-enforceable rights
opt_d: Only Spending-Clause statutes that expressly permit private suits are § 1983-enforceable
explanation: Talevski reaffirmed Gonzaga's rights-creating-language test and held that the Federal Nursing Home Reform Act met it; Spending-Clause origin is not categorically disqualifying.

stem: Harlow v. Fitzgerald replaced the prior subjective good-faith standard with:
opt_a: An objective test: whether a reasonable officer would have known the conduct violated clearly established law
opt_b: A purely procedural test focusing on whether qualified-immunity was raised early
opt_c: A balancing test weighing the right's importance against enforcement costs
opt_d: A categorical rule extending absolute immunity to all discretionary acts
explanation: Harlow's objective test — clearly established law a reasonable officer would have known — defines modern qualified immunity.

stem: Pearson v. Callahan modified Saucier v. Katz by holding that lower courts:
opt_a: May decide either prong of the qualified-immunity inquiry first
opt_b: Lack discretion to grant summary judgment on QI grounds before trial
opt_c: Must defer to the prosecutor's good-faith assessment of clearly established law
opt_d: Must always decide the constitutional question first
explanation: Pearson made Saucier's mandatory two-step optional, allowing courts to skip the constitutional question if QI is otherwise clear.

stem: Prosecutors have absolute immunity for advocacy functions but only qualified immunity for investigative tasks. The line between them was articulated in:
opt_a: Imbler v. Pachtman and Buckley v. Fitzsimmons
opt_b: Saucier v. Katz and Pearson v. Callahan
opt_c: Stump v. Sparkman and Pierson v. Ray
opt_d: Harlow v. Fitzgerald and Anderson v. Creighton
explanation: Imbler established absolute prosecutorial immunity for advocacy; Buckley clarified that pre-charge investigative work falls outside that protection.

stem: Trump v. United States (2024) held that the President's criminal immunity:
opt_a: Is absolute for all conduct during the presidential term
opt_b: Depends on whether the conduct was authorized by the Vice President
opt_c: Does not exist; Presidents may be prosecuted for any act
opt_d: Is absolute for core official acts and presumptive for other official conduct
explanation: Trump v. United States announced an absolute/presumptive structure for criminal immunity: absolute for core constitutional functions, presumptive for outer official conduct, none for unofficial acts.

stem: Under AEDPA § 2254(d)(1), federal habeas relief from a state-court adjudication on the merits requires that the state decision was:
opt_a: Erroneous, regardless of whether reasonable jurists could disagree
opt_b: Contrary to or an unreasonable application of clearly established federal law
opt_c: Based on an interpretation of state law inconsistent with federal precedent
opt_d: Subject to plain-error review under federal procedural standards
explanation: § 2254(d)(1) sets up a deferential 'contrary to / unreasonable application' standard, not de novo review.

stem: Williams v. Taylor distinguished 'contrary to' from 'unreasonable application' as follows:
opt_a: 'Contrary to' is reviewed de novo; 'unreasonable application' is reviewed for clear error
opt_b: The two prongs are functionally identical and should be treated as one
opt_c: 'Contrary to' applies to facts; 'unreasonable application' applies to law
opt_d: 'Contrary to' applies when the state court reached a conclusion opposite to clearly established law on materially indistinguishable facts; 'unreasonable application' applies when the state court correctly identified the rule but applied it unreasonably
explanation: Williams' canonical articulation of the AEDPA standards. 'Contrary to' = wrong rule or opposite outcome on indistinguishable facts. 'Unreasonable application' = right rule, unreasonable application.

stem: Cullen v. Pinholster restricted federal habeas review under § 2254(d)(1) to:
opt_a: The federal-habeas record, including evidence developed in federal court
opt_b: The combined state and federal records, with deference to state findings
opt_c: The record before the state court at the time of its merits adjudication
opt_d: Any record developed in subsequent state collateral review
explanation: Pinholster: when a state court adjudicated on the merits, federal § 2254(d)(1) review is limited to the state-court record, sharply curtailing new-evidence development.

stem: Wainwright v. Sykes replaced Fay v. Noia's deliberate-bypass standard with:
opt_a: AEDPA's deference standard
opt_b: A discretionary 'interests of justice' test
opt_c: A pure exhaustion requirement
opt_d: A cause-and-prejudice standard for procedural defaults
explanation: Wainwright tightened the procedural-default doctrine: petitioners must show external cause and actual prejudice (or a fundamental miscarriage gateway).

stem: Coleman v. Thompson held that attorney error in:
opt_a: Direct appeal automatically constitutes cause for default
opt_b: Capital sentencing automatically constitutes cause regardless of stage
opt_c: State collateral proceedings is generally not 'cause' for procedural default, because there is no constitutional right to counsel there
opt_d: Federal habeas proceedings is cause for default of state-court issues
explanation: Coleman: no constitutional right to counsel in state collateral review means attorney error there isn't 'cause' (Martinez carved a narrow exception).

stem: Martinez v. Ryan recognizes a narrow cause exception when:
opt_a: State direct-review counsel failed to raise an issue on direct appeal
opt_b: The petitioner was unrepresented during trial
opt_c: The federal habeas court concludes the conviction was clearly erroneous
opt_d: A state requires an ineffective-assistance claim to be raised first on initial-review collateral, and counsel was ineffective there
explanation: Martinez's narrow exception: where state procedural rules effectively make initial-review collateral the only forum for IATC claims, ineffective collateral-review counsel can be 'cause.'

stem: Under Teague v. Lane, a 'new rule' is generally not applied retroactively on collateral review except for:
opt_a: Rules announced by the Supreme Court (but not lower federal courts)
opt_b: Rules expressly designated retroactive by Congress
opt_c: Any rule announced after the conviction became final
opt_d: Substantive rules placing certain conduct beyond the criminal law's reach, and rare watershed procedural rules
explanation: Teague's two narrow exceptions: substantive rules and watershed procedural rules. Watershed rules have been almost theoretical.

stem: Stone v. Powell bars federal habeas review of:
opt_a: Fourth Amendment exclusionary-rule claims, where the state provided a full and fair opportunity to litigate
opt_b: Sixth Amendment claims raised for the first time on collateral review
opt_c: Fifth Amendment self-incrimination claims after Miranda
opt_d: All Fourth Amendment claims regardless of state-court procedure
explanation: Stone's narrow rule: Fourth Amendment exclusionary claims are not cognizable on habeas if the state provided full and fair litigation opportunity.

stem: McQuiggin v. Perkins held that a credible actual-innocence showing:
opt_a: Requires DNA evidence, not just affidavits
opt_b: Operates only to excuse procedural default, not the AEDPA SOL
opt_c: Is unavailable as a gateway under modern habeas doctrine
opt_d: Excuses AEDPA's one-year statute of limitations
explanation: McQuiggin extended Schlup's actual-innocence gateway to AEDPA's SOL: a credible innocence claim equitably tolls the limitations period.

stem: Boumediene v. Bush held that:
opt_a: Habeas is unavailable to non-citizens
opt_b: The Detainee Treatment Act provides an adequate substitute for habeas in all cases
opt_c: The Suspension Clause limits Congress's ability to strip habeas jurisdiction over Guantanamo detainees
opt_d: The Suspension Clause has no extraterritorial reach
explanation: Boumediene held that Guantanamo detainees enjoy Suspension Clause-protected habeas, and the MCA's substitute review was inadequate.

stem: A federal employee files a § 1983-style suit alleging that her supervisor's general workplace policies harm 'all employees.' She does not allege any concrete consequence to herself. The strongest standing argument against her is:
opt_a: She failed to exhaust administrative remedies before filing suit
opt_b: Her supervisor enjoys absolute immunity under federal-employment law
opt_c: Her injury is shared by all federal employees alike — a generalized grievance
opt_d: The political question doctrine bars review of internal personnel matters
explanation: Generalized grievances shared by the public (or here, all employees) lack the particularization Article III requires. The other options misframe the doctrinal hook.

stem: During a class-action challenge to an unconstitutional probation policy, the named plaintiff completes probation. The class has not yet been certified. The court should:
opt_a: Treat the class as automatically certified upon the named plaintiff's mootness
opt_b: Dismiss the entire action as moot because the named plaintiff has no live claim
opt_c: Order the state to identify a substitute plaintiff before considering certification
opt_d: Apply 'relation back' principles and consider whether the dispute is capable of repetition yet evading review
explanation: Sosna and progeny: a moot named plaintiff's claim can 'relate back' to certification, especially where the dispute is short-duration and recurring (capable of repetition exception).

stem: A national labor union files a pre-enforcement challenge against a new statute requiring members to disclose political contributions. No member has yet been prosecuted. The court should find the case ripe:
opt_a: If members face a credible threat of prosecution that chills their First Amendment activity
opt_b: Only after the first prosecution under the statute
opt_c: Whenever the legal question is purely one of statutory interpretation
opt_d: Only if the union can identify members who plan to violate
explanation: SBA List v. Driehaus permits pre-enforcement First Amendment challenges where credible threat plus chilling effect on protected conduct are alleged.

stem: An environmental NGO sues on behalf of unnamed members alleging harm to a national park. To establish associational standing, the group must show:
opt_a: Members would have standing in their own right; the interest is germane to the org's purpose; relief does not require individual member participation
opt_b: Each member has individually demonstrated injury, traceability, and redressability
opt_c: The organization has incorporated and registered as a 501(c)(3)
opt_d: The membership has formally voted to authorize the litigation
explanation: Hunt v. Washington Apple's three-prong test for associational standing. Individual injury showings are NOT required if all three Hunt factors are met.

stem: A consumer sues a credit reporting agency under FCRA, alleging the agency maintained inaccurate information about her in its files. She does not allege the inaccuracy was ever transmitted to a third party. After Spokeo and TransUnion, she:
opt_a: Has standing because Congress provided a private right of action under FCRA
opt_b: Lacks standing because the harm has no close historical analog absent publication
opt_c: Has standing only if she can prove the inaccuracy caused emotional distress
opt_d: Has standing because the inaccurate file is itself a concrete intangible injury
explanation: TransUnion: even with a Congressionally-created cause of action, the plaintiff must show concrete harm with a close historical or common-law analog. Retention without publication has no defamation analog.

stem: A coastal state sues the federal government to challenge an EPA rule that allegedly under-regulates emissions, citing Massachusetts v. EPA's 'special solicitude.' Post-Massachusetts cases suggest the state's argument:
opt_a: Will succeed automatically because states inherently have standing
opt_b: Will face skepticism; special solicitude has not been broadly extended
opt_c: Will fail because climate-change harms are nonjusticiable political questions
opt_d: Depends on whether Congress expressly authorized the state to sue
explanation: Massachusetts v. EPA's 'special solicitude' has been read narrowly in subsequent cases (e.g., AHM, Murthy). States get some deference but not a categorical pass.

stem: Parents of public-school students sue the IRS, alleging it should deny tax-exempt status to private schools that discriminate. Allen v. Wright held the parents lacked standing because:
opt_a: Tax matters are nonjusticiable political questions
opt_b: The IRS enjoys sovereign immunity from such suits
opt_c: Parents lack third-party standing to assert their children's interests
opt_d: The chain of causation runs through independent third-party private-school decisions and parental choices
explanation: Allen v. Wright: even granting injury, the chain to IRS action runs through independent third-party choices, breaking traceability.

stem: Doctors challenge FDA's relaxation of mifepristone access rules, alleging they may someday treat patients harmed by complications. After AHM v. FDA (2024):
opt_a: The political question doctrine forecloses review of FDA scientific judgments
opt_b: The doctors' professional interest in medication safety is itself a concrete injury
opt_c: Article III generalized-grievance limits bar all such challenges
opt_d: The doctors' relationship to harm is too speculative to support standing
explanation: Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine: the chain from FDA approval to potential future patient harm to the plaintiff doctors was too speculative.

stem: A wilderness society challenges a Forest Service regulation arguing that the agency violated the National Environmental Policy Act's procedural requirements. Under Lujan / Summers v. Earth Island, procedural injury alone:
opt_a: Is insufficient without an underlying concrete interest the procedure was designed to protect
opt_b: Is sufficient where Congress has provided procedural rights by statute
opt_c: Is sufficient if the plaintiff is within the statute's zone of interests
opt_d: Suffices for standing whenever an agency violates rulemaking procedures
explanation: Summers v. Earth Island: a 'free-floating' procedural injury is insufficient; the plaintiff must show a concrete underlying interest the procedure was meant to protect.

stem: A federal court is asked whether a treaty interpretation is governed by political-question doctrine. Under Baker v. Carr, the relevant factors include:
opt_a: Whether the executive has promulgated implementing regulations
opt_b: Whether the case implicates state sovereignty or federal supremacy
opt_c: Whether the treaty was ratified by both chambers of Congress
opt_d: Textual commitment to a coordinate political branch and lack of judicially manageable standards
explanation: Baker v. Carr's six factors: textual commitment, lack of standards, impossibility without policy determination, lack of respect for coordinate branches, need for finality, embarrassment from multifarious pronouncements.

stem: Congress passes a statute that strips federal courts of jurisdiction over any case challenging executive immigration decisions. State courts retain jurisdiction. Under modern doctrine, the strongest argument for the statute:
opt_a: The Exceptions Clause provides plenary authority to strip any jurisdiction
opt_b: Article III is non-self-executing for matters Congress chooses to remove
opt_c: Klein-style scrutiny does not apply outside criminal cases
opt_d: Sheldon v. Sill permits Congress to limit lower-court jurisdiction whenever state-court alternatives exist
explanation: Where state courts retain a forum, the central forum-stripping concern dissolves; Sheldon v. Sill's logic supports the statute. Klein still applies to outcome-rigging.

stem: In a bankruptcy proceeding, a debtor's spouse files a creditor's claim and the trustee asserts a state-law fraudulent-transfer counterclaim that requires the same factual findings as the claim's validity. Under Stern:
opt_a: The bankruptcy court must convert the proceeding to a non-core matter and submit recommendations
opt_b: The bankruptcy court lacks any authority over the state-law counterclaim
opt_c: The bankruptcy court may enter final judgment on the counterclaim because it is necessarily resolved by the claim-allowance process
opt_d: The parties must consent in writing for the bankruptcy court to enter final judgment
explanation: Stern's exception: when the counterclaim is necessarily resolved as part of claim allowance, bankruptcy courts may enter final judgment.

stem: A federal statute classifies certain bankruptcy proceedings as 'core' and others as 'non-core.' For non-core matters, the bankruptcy court:
opt_a: Loses jurisdiction entirely; the matter must be filed in district court
opt_b: May enter final judgment if no party objects within 14 days
opt_c: Submits proposed findings of fact and conclusions of law to a district judge for de novo review
opt_d: May enter final judgment with appellate review limited to abuse of discretion
explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 157: non-core matters get district-judge review of bankruptcy-court proposed findings, preserving Article III's essential attributes.

stem: An agency adjudicates whether a vessel collision occurred within its regulatory scope. The 'jurisdictional fact' doctrine of Crowell v. Benson permits:
opt_a: No Article III review at all if the agency's procedures comport with due process
opt_b: Article III review only if the parties consent
opt_c: Article III review de novo on facts that condition the agency's authority
opt_d: Plenary Article III review of all factual findings
explanation: Crowell required de novo Article III review of 'jurisdictional facts' that condition agency authority. The doctrine has been narrowed but remains the framework.

stem: After Wellness International v. Sharif, parties may consent to a bankruptcy judge's final adjudication of a Stern claim. Consent is valid only if it is:
opt_a: Express and in writing, executed before the proceeding begins
opt_b: Inferred from the parties' failure to object to bankruptcy-court adjudication
opt_c: Knowing and voluntary, with awareness of the right to Article III review
opt_d: Approved by the district judge after a colloquy with counsel
explanation: Wellness: consent must be 'knowing and voluntary,' which can be implied from conduct but requires awareness of the right being waived.

stem: Congress enacts a statute providing that 'no court of the United States shall consider any claim that the President's discharge of military advisors was unlawful, where such discharge has been certified by the Secretary of Defense.' The statute is most likely:
opt_a: Subject to Klein scrutiny because it appears to dictate outcomes through jurisdictional language
opt_b: Valid because military matters are public rights under Crowell
opt_c: Plainly valid under the Exceptions Clause as a categorical jurisdictional withdrawal
opt_d: Plainly invalid because Congress cannot affect any military matters
explanation: The certification mechanism resembles Klein's outcome-rigging — the statute essentially commands a result through jurisdictional language. The statute would face Klein-style scrutiny.

stem: Congress repeals all Supreme Court appellate jurisdiction over a category of habeas cases while litigation is pending. Under McCardle and Boumediene:
opt_a: The Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction is constitutionally fixed and not subject to congressional repeal
opt_b: The repeal is generally valid under the Exceptions Clause, but the Suspension Clause may protect underlying habeas rights
opt_c: Such repeals are categorically barred because they manipulate ongoing litigation
opt_d: Such repeals are valid only if Congress provides an alternate adjudicative forum
explanation: McCardle established broad Exceptions-Clause power to repeal SCOTUS appellate jurisdiction; Boumediene confirmed the Suspension Clause as an external limit.

stem: In a federal civil trial, the magistrate judge presides over a dispositive summary-judgment motion with the parties' written consent. Under 28 U.S.C. § 636(c):
opt_a: The magistrate's authority is limited to non-dispositive matters even with consent
opt_b: The district judge must enter the magistrate's recommended judgment as a final order
opt_c: The magistrate may enter final judgment, with appellate review going directly to the court of appeals
opt_d: Either party retains a unilateral right to demand district-judge review of the magistrate's ruling
explanation: § 636(c) allows magistrates to enter final judgment with party consent; appeals go directly to the circuit court, bypassing the district judge.

stem: A federal statute reduces the salary of all sitting Article III judges by 10% to fund pandemic relief. The statute is:
opt_a: Unconstitutional under Article III § 1's compensation guarantee
opt_b: Constitutional if applied prospectively to all judges including future appointees
opt_c: Subject to Article III scrutiny only if applied retroactively
opt_d: Constitutional under Congress's spending power and necessary-and-proper authority
explanation: The Compensation Clause forbids diminishment 'during their continuance in office' for sitting judges. Prospective rules for future judges are different.

stem: A federal statute requires district-court magistrates to issue advisory opinions on the constitutionality of proposed agency rules before they take effect. The statute:
opt_a: Is valid if magistrates' opinions are advisory and non-binding on later litigation
opt_b: Is valid because magistrates are not Article III judges
opt_c: Is valid if the agency may decline to follow the magistrate's opinion
opt_d: Likely violates the prohibition on advisory opinions and Article III's case-or-controversy requirement
explanation: Echoes Hayburn's Case: federal judges (and their adjuncts) cannot perform non-judicial functions outside concrete adversarial disputes.

stem: Plaintiff brings a state-law shareholder action alleging the corporation's investment in federally-issued bonds was ultra vires because the federal authorizing statute is unconstitutional. The federal-question issue:
opt_a: Supports FQJ only if the federal government is joined as a party
opt_b: Defeats FQJ under Merrell Dow because there is no federal private right of action
opt_c: Cannot support FQJ because the cause of action is created by state corporate law
opt_d: Is necessarily raised, actually disputed, and substantial — supporting FQJ under Smith v. Kansas City Title
explanation: Smith v. Kansas City Title remains the canonical example of substantial-federal-issue FQJ. Constitutional validity of a federal statute is precisely the kind of substantial issue Grable preserves.

stem: Under the Holmes rule, federal-question jurisdiction is sufficient (though not necessary) when:
opt_a: Federal preemption is implicated by the underlying contract
opt_b: Federal law creates the cause of action plaintiff asserts
opt_c: The plaintiff's claim implicates a federal-law issue at any point
opt_d: The defendant raises a federal-law defense in the answer
explanation: Holmes' rule (American Well Works): federal law creating the cause of action is a sufficient test, but not the only path (Smith/Grable supplements).

stem: § 1331 has been interpreted more narrowly than Article III's 'arising under' language because:
opt_a: The Supreme Court strictly construes congressional jurisdictional grants
opt_b: § 1331 incorporates the well-pleaded complaint rule, while Article III reaches any case with a federal ingredient
opt_c: Article III does not impose any limit on Congressional jurisdictional choices
opt_d: § 1331 was enacted before the modern federal-question doctrine took shape
explanation: Osborn read Article III broadly; § 1331 is read more narrowly via Mottley's well-pleaded complaint rule.

stem: Plaintiff brings a federal antitrust claim and joins three state-law unfair-competition claims arising from the same defendant's conduct. The federal court:
opt_a: Has supplemental jurisdiction only if the parties stipulate to federal venue
opt_b: Lacks supplemental jurisdiction because state-law claims must be filed separately
opt_c: Has supplemental jurisdiction only if state law specifically authorizes joinder
opt_d: Has supplemental jurisdiction over the state claims under § 1367(a) because they share a common nucleus
explanation: § 1367(a) codifies Gibbs's common-nucleus test; closely related state claims are within supplemental jurisdiction.

stem: In a diversity case, P (NY) sues D (CA). D impleads a third-party defendant TPD (NY) under FRCP 14. P then asserts a direct claim against TPD. Under § 1367(b):
opt_a: Supplemental jurisdiction extends to P's claim because TPD was already in the case
opt_b: P may proceed against TPD because § 1367(b) only limits federal-question cases
opt_c: P's claim against TPD is barred because complete diversity would be destroyed
opt_d: TPD may consent to jurisdiction, curing any diversity defect
explanation: § 1367(b) codifies Kroger: in diversity cases, plaintiffs cannot use FRCP 14/19/20/24 joinder to evade complete diversity.

stem: P (NY) sues D (NY) on a federal claim plus a related state claim, in NY federal court. Under § 1441(b):
opt_a: Removal would be available under § 1441(b) by either party
opt_b: § 1441(b)'s forum-defendant rule bars federal jurisdiction
opt_c: Removal would not have been available because there is no diversity, but the case may be filed originally as FQJ
opt_d: § 1441(b) is irrelevant because the action originates in federal court
explanation: § 1441(b) is a removal rule, not an original-filing rule. P can file directly in federal court because § 1331 supplies FQJ. The forum-defendant rule limits removal of diversity cases when the defendant is a forum citizen.

stem: For diversity-jurisdiction purposes, a corporation incorporated in Delaware with its headquarters and most administrative direction in Texas is a citizen of:
opt_a: Whichever state has more employees, under § 1332(c)
opt_b: Delaware and Texas, under Hertz Corp. v. Friend's nerve-center test
opt_c: Texas only, because principal place of business is the modern test
opt_d: Delaware only, because state of incorporation is the historical test
explanation: Hertz: a corporation is a citizen of its state of incorporation AND the state where its 'nerve center' (headquarters/management) is located.

stem: A nationwide class action with 5,000 members from 38 states alleges a single defendant violated state consumer-protection laws. The aggregate amount in controversy exceeds $50M. The defendant:
opt_a: May remove only with the consent of a majority of class members
opt_b: May remove only if a federal-question is presented
opt_c: May remove under CAFA because of minimal diversity and the $5M threshold
opt_d: Must remain in state court because state-law class actions are non-removable
explanation: CAFA permits removal of class actions with minimal diversity (any class member differs from any defendant) and aggregate amount over $5M.

stem: Exxon Mobil v. Allapattah held that in a diversity class action, supplemental jurisdiction extends to:
opt_a: Class members who joined after class certification
opt_b: Class members whose individual damages fall below the $75,000 amount-in-controversy threshold
opt_c: Class members whose claims arise under different state laws
opt_d: Class members who are citizens of the same state as a defendant
explanation: Exxon Mobil: § 1367(a) reaches class members below the amount-in-controversy threshold so long as at least one named plaintiff meets it.

stem: Under 28 U.S.C. § 1446(b), a defendant must generally file a notice of removal within:
opt_a: 90 days after the case is filed in state court
opt_b: 60 days after service of the complaint
opt_c: 30 days after receipt of the initial pleading or the amended pleading making the case removable
opt_d: 6 months after the cause of action accrues
explanation: § 1446(b)(1): 30-day removal window from receipt of the initial pleading. § 1446(b)(3) provides a separate 30-day window for cases that become removable later.

stem: A state court holds that a search violated 'the Fourth Amendment as we have interpreted it.' The court does not invoke any state-constitutional ground. The U.S. Supreme Court should:
opt_a: Review under § 1257 because the decision rests squarely on federal law
opt_b: Decline review under the adequate-and-independent state ground doctrine
opt_c: Vacate and remand under Capital Cities for clarification of state-law basis
opt_d: Apply Murdock to limit review to federal questions only
explanation: Where the state court relies exclusively on federal law, there is no adequate state ground; review proceeds.

stem: A state court holds that 'because the federal Fourth Amendment requires this result under our reading of Mapp v. Ohio, we hold the search invalid; we do not reach the state constitution.' Under Michigan v. Long:
opt_a: Review proceeds only if the state-court interpretation is reasonable
opt_b: Review proceeds because the state court did not invoke an independent state-law ground
opt_c: Review is barred because the federal-law interpretation is binding on the state court
opt_d: Review is barred because the state court declined to consider state law
explanation: Long's presumption: the state court rested on federal law; review is permitted because state law was disclaimed.

stem: A state court issues an opinion citing both state and federal constitutional grounds without specifying which controls. The U.S. Supreme Court has the option to:
opt_a: Apply Long's presumption and review on the merits
opt_b: Apply de novo review of the federal-law portion only
opt_c: Vacate and remand for the state court to clarify whether its decision rests on adequate independent state grounds
opt_d: Decline review automatically under Fox Films
explanation: Capital Cities Media v. Toole: vacate-and-remand for clarification is one of the Court's tools when a state-court opinion is ambiguous about the basis for decision.

stem: A state court holds that under state contract law, no valid contract was formed between a teacher and the school district; therefore, the dismissal could not have impaired any contract under the federal Contracts Clause. The Supreme Court:
opt_a: May review only under Long's presumption when state and federal law are intertwined
opt_b: May review the state-law contract question because it is antecedent to the federal Contracts Clause analysis
opt_c: Must defer entirely to the state-court interpretation of state contract law
opt_d: Lacks jurisdiction because the state-law ground is independent and adequate
explanation: Indiana ex rel. Anderson v. Brand: the Supreme Court may review state-law antecedents that determine federal rights, with deference but not absolute.

stem: A defendant raises a federal Confrontation Clause challenge for the first time in his petition for certiorari to the Supreme Court, having waived it in state appeals. The Court should:
opt_a: Review the claim because federal preservation rules differ from state preservation rules
opt_b: Remand to state court for first-instance consideration of the unpreserved claim
opt_c: Review the claim under plain-error principles
opt_d: Decline review under Cardinale because the federal claim was not raised below
explanation: Cardinale v. Louisiana: failure to raise the federal claim below generally bars Supreme Court review.

stem: A state court holds the NAACP in contempt for refusing to disclose membership lists, applying a procedural rule that the state has never previously applied to civil-rights organizations. The Supreme Court:
opt_a: May review because a novel procedural ground that obstructs federal rights is inadequate
opt_b: Lacks jurisdiction because membership lists are exclusively a matter of state law
opt_c: Must defer to the state procedural ruling under the adequate-and-independent doctrine
opt_d: Should remand for reconsideration of the procedural question
explanation: NAACP v. Alabama: novel applications of state procedural rules — particularly when used against unpopular litigants — are not adequate to bar federal review.

stem: Under Testa v. Katt, a state court must:
opt_a: Apply federal procedural rules whenever federal law is implicated
opt_b: Enforce non-discriminatory federal claims, treating them as it would analogous state claims
opt_c: Defer to federal-court interpretations of any federal statute
opt_d: Refer all federal-question cases to federal court
explanation: Testa: state courts cannot refuse jurisdiction over federal claims absent a neutral, non-discriminatory excuse.

stem: Wisconsin requires § 1983 plaintiffs to give 120 days' notice to a state agency before filing suit; no analogous notice requirement applies to comparable state-law claims. Under Felder v. Casey:
opt_a: The state notice requirement is preempted in § 1983 actions because it is outcome-determinative and burdens federal rights
opt_b: The state notice requirement is permissible if it provides meaningful exhaustion
opt_c: The state notice requirement applies only in state court but not federal court
opt_d: The state notice requirement applies because procedural rules are matters of state sovereignty
explanation: Felder v. Casey: state procedural rules that burden § 1983 enforcement and are outcome-determinative are preempted, even in state court.

stem: A state court applies its state-law sovereign-immunity doctrine to dismiss a § 1983 claim against a state official sued in personal capacity. Under Howlett v. Rose:
opt_a: The state-law immunity controls because § 1983 incorporates state-law immunities by reference
opt_b: The dismissal is permissible only if Congress expressly waived state immunity in § 1983
opt_c: The state-law immunity is preempted; § 1983 personal-capacity suits cannot be defeated by state sovereign-immunity doctrines
opt_d: The state-law immunity applies only if the official acted in official capacity
explanation: Howlett v. Rose: state courts cannot use state-law immunities to defeat § 1983 personal-capacity suits; federal law governs § 1983 immunity questions.

stem: In an appeal from a state court, the U.S. Supreme Court will defer to the state court's interpretation of state law:
opt_a: Only when the state-court interpretation is unanimous
opt_b: In all circumstances; state courts are the final arbiters of state law
opt_c: Only when the federal-law question is also unsettled
opt_d: Generally, except where the state-law question is antecedent to a federal right or used to evade federal review
explanation: General rule of state-law deference (Murdock), with narrow exceptions for antecedent issues affecting federal rights (Brand) and evasion (NAACP).

stem: In a diversity action filed in federal court in Pennsylvania, the plaintiff's claim accrued in 2018; PA's 2-year SOL would bar the claim, while a federal default would not. Under Erie:
opt_a: The court applies federal common-law SOL because the case is in federal court
opt_b: The court applies the SOL of whichever state has the most-significant relationship
opt_c: The court must apply PA's SOL because state SOLs are substantive for Erie purposes
opt_d: The court applies the more permissive federal default to give the plaintiff her day in court
explanation: Guaranty Trust v. York: state SOLs are 'substantive' for Erie, even though they look procedural, because they are outcome-determinative.

stem: In federal court in California, a plaintiff serves the defendant in a manner authorized by FRCP 4(e) but not by California's service rules. Under Hanna v. Plumer:
opt_a: The court applies the rule less favorable to the plaintiff to discourage forum shopping
opt_b: California's service rule controls because service is procedural
opt_c: The plaintiff must comply with both rules to avoid an outcome-determinative effect
opt_d: FRCP 4(e) controls because it is a valid REA-authorized rule
explanation: Hanna's two-track: FRCP rules valid under the REA control over conflicting state law without further outcome-determinative analysis.

stem: The federal government issues a treasury check to A; B steals the check, forges A's endorsement, and cashes it at Bank C. The U.S. sues C for conversion. The applicable rule of decision:
opt_a: Is determined by the bank's deposit agreement with C
opt_b: Depends on whether the federal government has waived sovereign immunity
opt_c: Is federal common law because of the federal interest in uniform U.S. commercial transactions
opt_d: Is the law of whichever state the check was negotiated in
explanation: Clearfield Trust: U.S. commercial paper is a federal-common-law enclave because of the federal interest in uniformity across the country.

stem: A defense contractor manufactures helicopters to government specifications; a marine drowns in a defective escape hatch. Under Boyle's three-part test, the contractor's defense requires showing:
opt_a: That the U.S. is a co-defendant in the suit
opt_b: Reasonably precise government specifications, conformance with them, and warning of dangers known to the contractor but not the government
opt_c: That the contractor was a federal agency or instrumentality
opt_d: That the helicopter was used in active combat operations
explanation: Boyle: federal common-law government-contractor defense requires (1) reasonably precise specs, (2) conformance, (3) warning of known dangers.

stem: In a federal lien-priority dispute under SBA programs, the federal interest in uniform liens does not require nationwide uniformity. Under Kimbell Foods, the court should:
opt_a: Adopt a uniform federal common-law priority rule based on first-filed
opt_b: Borrow state law as the federal rule, applying the state UCC priorities
opt_c: Apply whichever rule produces the larger recovery for the federal government
opt_d: Refer the question to the SBA for an administrative priority determination
explanation: Kimbell Foods: where uniformity is not essential, courts borrow state law as the federal rule to avoid disrupting commercial expectations.

stem: A bank holds a promissory note from a borrower with an undisclosed side agreement that the bank will not enforce the note. The bank fails; FDIC takes over. The borrower asserts the side agreement against FDIC. Under D'Oench Duhme:
opt_a: FDIC steps into the bank's shoes and is bound by the side agreement
opt_b: State contract law governs the side agreement's enforceability
opt_c: FDIC's interest is governed by federal common law, which protects FDIC against unwritten side agreements
opt_d: The side agreement is enforceable only if recorded with the FDIC at takeover
explanation: D'Oench Duhme: federal common law protects FDIC's interest in commercial paper against undisclosed side agreements that would weaken regulatory examination.

stem: § 301 of the LMRA provides federal-court jurisdiction over labor-contract suits but does not specify substantive rules. Lincoln Mills held:
opt_a: § 301 incorporates the law of the state where the contract was negotiated
opt_b: § 301 is unconstitutional under Article III because it lacks a substantive federal-law basis
opt_c: § 301 authorizes federal courts to develop federal common law of labor contracts
opt_d: § 301 confers jurisdiction only; substantive law is governed by Erie
explanation: Textile Workers v. Lincoln Mills: § 301's jurisdictional grant implicitly authorized federal common-law development. Frankfurter dissented on Article III grounds.

stem: In Texas Industries v. Radcliff Materials, the Supreme Court declined to recognize an implied federal common-law right of contribution among antitrust violators because:
opt_a: Antitrust contribution is a uniquely state-law matter
opt_b: The Sherman Act preempts all state-law contribution rights
opt_c: Federal common law is disfavored where Congress has chosen not to provide the right and a comprehensive statutory scheme exists
opt_d: Federal common law cannot create rights that affect private commercial conduct
explanation: Texas Industries: the Court is reluctant to expand federal common law absent clear federal interest and need; a comprehensive statutory scheme cuts against extension.

stem: A Cuban government expropriates sugar in Cuba; a U.S. importer claims the expropriation was invalid under international law. The federal court hearing the U.S. importer's suit:
opt_a: Applies the act-of-state doctrine as federal common law, generally barring inquiry into the validity of the foreign sovereign's act
opt_b: Applies state choice-of-law rules to determine the applicable substantive law
opt_c: Treats the expropriation question as a political question outside judicial competence
opt_d: Defers to State Department certifications about the legality of the expropriation
explanation: Banco Nacional v. Sabbatino: the act-of-state doctrine is federal common law arising from the federal interest in foreign relations.

stem: Erie's 'twin aims' under Hanna v. Plumer are:
opt_a: Protecting jury rights and avoiding inconsistent verdicts
opt_b: Promoting judicial economy and respecting party autonomy
opt_c: Discouraging forum-shopping and avoiding inequitable administration of laws between federal and state courts
opt_d: Preserving state sovereignty and federal supremacy
explanation: Hanna's framing: Erie aims to (1) prevent forum-shopping incentives that attract litigants to federal court and (2) avoid arbitrary outcome differences between federal and state courts.

stem: Bivens supplies a federal damages remedy for constitutional violations by federal officers. After Egbert v. Boule, this remedy is available:
opt_a: Whenever the plaintiff can show no special factors counsel hesitation
opt_b: In any case involving an enumerated constitutional right
opt_c: Almost exclusively in the original Bivens (4A search), Davis (5A gender), and Carlson (8A medical) contexts
opt_d: In any case where alternative remedies are inadequate
explanation: Egbert effectively forecloses Bivens extensions; only the three core contexts remain reliably available.

stem: A Congressional staffer alleges her supervisor fired her because of her gender. Title VII excludes Congressional staff. Under Davis v. Passman:
opt_a: § 1983 provides the cause of action because Congressional staff are state actors
opt_b: The staffer's exclusive remedy is administrative review by the House or Senate ethics committee
opt_c: Bivens supplies an implied damages remedy for the Fifth Amendment due-process gender-discrimination claim
opt_d: No damages remedy is available because Title VII's exclusion forecloses any private right
explanation: Davis: when Title VII expressly excluded Congressional staff, leaving them with no other remedy, Bivens filled the gap.

stem: A federal inmate dies of asthma after prison medical staff repeatedly ignore his symptoms. His estate sues under Bivens for an Eighth Amendment violation. Under Carlson v. Green:
opt_a: Bivens applies only to the medical staff personally, not the warden
opt_b: The FTCA's exclusive-remedy provisions foreclose Bivens
opt_c: Bivens damages remain available, even though the FTCA also provides a remedy, because uniform federal damages serve constitutional values
opt_d: The estate must first exhaust BOP administrative remedies under the PLRA
explanation: Carlson: FTCA does not displace Bivens; uniform federal damages directly against officers serve different functions.

stem: A female applicant is denied admission to a state medical school that she alleges violates Title IX. Under Cannon v. University of Chicago:
opt_a: Title IX private suits are foreclosed because the statute provides administrative remedies
opt_b: The applicant's exclusive remedy is administrative complaint to OCR
opt_c: Title IX private suits are available only with the Secretary of Education's authorization
opt_d: Title IX implicitly creates a private right of action for intentional sex discrimination
explanation: Cannon: Title IX includes an implied PROA for intentional discrimination; this remains good law post-Sandoval (which limited disparate-impact claims).

stem: Alabama administers driver's license tests only in English. A non-English-speaking applicant sues, alleging this disparately impacts national-origin minorities under Title VI's regulations. After Sandoval:
opt_a: The applicant's exclusive remedy is administrative complaint to DOJ
opt_b: Private parties may sue if the agency promulgating the regulations expressly authorized
opt_c: Private parties may sue because Title VI's regulations clearly intend private enforcement
opt_d: No private right of action exists for disparate-impact claims under Title VI's regulations
explanation: Sandoval: only Congress, not an agency through regulation, can create a private right of action; Title VI's text supports only intentional-discrimination PROAs.

stem: A federal Border Patrol agent stops a U.S. citizen at an inland checkpoint and uses excessive force. The citizen sues under Bivens for a Fourth Amendment violation. After Egbert v. Boule:
opt_a: The claim depends on whether the inland checkpoint qualifies as 'border' for these purposes
opt_b: The claim succeeds because the FTCA does not provide an adequate alternative
opt_c: The court will likely decline to extend Bivens because any rational reason for Congressional preference defeats extension
opt_d: The claim falls within Bivens's core 4A context and proceeds without difficulty
explanation: Egbert's collapsed test makes nearly all Bivens extensions fail. Even Fourth Amendment claims face headwinds outside the original Bivens factual context.

stem: A U.S. Border Patrol agent shoots and kills a Mexican national across the border. Family members sue under Bivens for the Fourth and Fifth Amendment violations. Under Hernandez v. Mesa:
opt_a: Bivens applies because U.S. constitutional rights extend extraterritorially to non-citizens
opt_b: No Bivens action lies because cross-border shootings present a new context with foreign-relations special factors
opt_c: Bivens applies because the agent's conduct fell within the Fourth Amendment's domestic reach
opt_d: The family must first exhaust diplomatic remedies through the State Department
explanation: Hernandez v. Mesa: cross-border context plus foreign-relations special factors defeat Bivens extension.

stem: After Sandoval, a federal court considering whether a statute creates an implied private right of action focuses primarily on:
opt_a: The four Cort v. Ash factors as the controlling framework
opt_b: Whether the statute leaves the plaintiff with no other meaningful remedy
opt_c: The statute's text and structure, asking whether Congress used rights-creating language and intended a private remedy
opt_d: The legislative history, agency interpretations, and policy considerations
explanation: Sandoval shifted the inquiry to text and structure, displacing Cort v. Ash's broader purposivist analysis.

stem: A NASA engineer is demoted for whistleblowing speech protected by the First Amendment. He sues under Bivens for damages. Under Bush v. Lucas:
opt_a: The engineer must elect between civil-service review and Bivens at the outset
opt_b: Bivens applies because First Amendment retaliation claims are core to the original holding
opt_c: No Bivens action lies because the federal civil-service remedial scheme provides adequate alternative review
opt_d: Bivens applies because the civil-service scheme does not provide damages
explanation: Bush v. Lucas: where Congress has provided an adequate alternative remedial scheme (here, civil-service review), Bivens does not extend.

stem: A rancher alleges that BLM officials systematically harassed him for refusing to grant easements over his land. He sues for damages under Bivens. Under Wilkie v. Robbins:
opt_a: Bivens applies because federal officers' targeted retaliation falls within the original holding
opt_b: The rancher's exclusive remedy is administrative review through the Department of the Interior
opt_c: Bivens applies because the harassment violated the rancher's Fifth Amendment due-process rights
opt_d: No Bivens action lies because the doctrine would be unworkable without a clear standard for unconstitutional retaliation in this context
explanation: Wilkie v. Robbins: 'special factors' include administrability — a freestanding retaliation claim required courts to police every federal-officer interaction with land users.

stem: A plaintiff sues a state attorney general in federal court seeking an injunction against ongoing enforcement of a state statute alleged to violate the federal Constitution. Ex parte Young permits this suit because:
opt_a: The 11th Amendment expressly authorizes officer suits against state officials
opt_b: Federal-question jurisdiction overrides state sovereign immunity in all civil rights cases
opt_c: The state's filing of an answer constitutes a waiver of sovereign immunity
opt_d: The officer enforcing an unconstitutional law is stripped of state immunity for purposes of prospective injunctive relief
explanation: Ex parte Young's officer-suit fiction: an officer enforcing an unconstitutional law acts ultra vires, losing the state's immunity shield for prospective relief.

stem: A plaintiff prevails in a federal-court Ex parte Young action and obtains an injunction requiring the state to provide back-due benefits to wrongly denied recipients. Under Edelman v. Jordan:
opt_a: The injunction is permissible because § 1983 abrogates state sovereign immunity
opt_b: The injunction is permissible because it is styled as compliance with federal law
opt_c: The injunction is permissible only if back-due benefits exceed $10,000
opt_d: The injunction's retroactive monetary component violates the 11th Amendment because it requires payment from the state treasury
explanation: Edelman v. Jordan: Ex parte Young permits prospective relief but bars retroactive monetary relief from the state treasury, even when styled as compliance.

stem: A class of disabled residents sues to compel state mental-health facilities to comply with state-law standards governing institutional conditions. Under Pennhurst v. Halderman:
opt_a: No federal-court Ex parte Young suit lies because federal courts cannot use Young to enforce state law against state officials
opt_b: Federal courts have inherent jurisdiction to ensure state compliance with state law
opt_c: Federal courts may enforce state law against state officials whenever the underlying claim is constitutional
opt_d: The supremacy clause permits federal-court enforcement of state law in civil-rights cases
explanation: Pennhurst v. Halderman: Young is for federal-law enforcement; using it to enforce state law against state officials would intrude on state sovereignty without supremacy-clause warrant.

stem: Under Seminole Tribe v. Florida, congressional abrogation of state sovereign immunity requires:
opt_a: An unequivocal expression of intent and a state's express consent
opt_b: A federal interest sufficient to displace state sovereign immunity
opt_c: An unequivocal expression of intent to abrogate AND exercise of a constitutional power that permits abrogation
opt_d: Article I commerce-power authority and § 5 enforcement together
explanation: Seminole Tribe's two-step: (1) clear statement of intent and (2) valid abrogation power. Article I commerce powers are insufficient; § 5 of the 14th Amendment works.

stem: An employee sues a state university under the Family and Medical Leave Act's family-care provision. Under Nevada Department of Human Resources v. Hibbs:
opt_a: The FMLA cannot abrogate state immunity because it is grounded in commerce power
opt_b: The FMLA's family-care provision is a valid § 5 abrogation because it remedies historical sex discrimination in family leave
opt_c: The employee's exclusive remedy is administrative review under state employment law
opt_d: The FMLA's abrogation is invalid because it lacks 'congruence and proportionality'
explanation: Hibbs: FMLA's family-care provision was upheld as valid § 5 abrogation, citing the history of sex discrimination Congress sought to remedy.

stem: A paraplegic plaintiff sues a state under Title II of the ADA, alleging the state's courthouse lacks accessibility. Under Tennessee v. Lane:
opt_a: Title II's abrogation is invalid because it exceeds § 5 'congruence and proportionality'
opt_b: The plaintiff's exclusive remedy is administrative complaint to DOJ
opt_c: Title II's abrogation is valid as applied because access to courts implicates fundamental rights
opt_d: Title II applies only to private accommodations, not state courthouses
explanation: Tennessee v. Lane: Title II abrogation is valid as applied to fundamental-rights cases like access to courts.

stem: A county prosecutor authorizes a particular police entry into a doctor's office, applying his understanding of the Fourth Amendment. The entry is later held unconstitutional. Under Pembaur v. Cincinnati:
opt_a: The county may be liable under § 1983 because the prosecutor's single decision constitutes county 'policy' if he is the final decisionmaker
opt_b: The prosecutor's authorization triggers absolute prosecutorial immunity for the county
opt_c: The county is liable only if a pattern of similar decisions exists
opt_d: Only the prosecutor personally is liable; the county is shielded by sovereign immunity
explanation: Pembaur: a single decision by a final policymaker can constitute Monell 'policy' for § 1983 municipal liability.

stem: A pretrial detainee suffers a medical emergency and is denied treatment by police; he sues the city for failure to train. Under City of Canton v. Harris:
opt_a: The city is liable on a respondeat-superior theory whenever its officers cause constitutional harm
opt_b: The city is liable only if the plaintiff identifies the specific officer responsible
opt_c: The city is liable only if the failure to train reflects deliberate indifference to a known risk
opt_d: The city is automatically liable because medical training is a known police duty
explanation: Canton: failure-to-train liability requires deliberate indifference — a known and substantial risk that training would have addressed.

stem: A wrongly convicted plaintiff alleges that prosecutors failed to disclose Brady material in his 1985 trial; he claims the city's failure to train prosecutors caused this single Brady violation. Under Connick v. Thompson:
opt_a: Single Brady violations always support failure-to-train liability because of the right's importance
opt_b: The plaintiff must instead pursue a malicious-prosecution theory against the prosecutors
opt_c: The plaintiff's claim is barred by absolute prosecutorial immunity, including for the city
opt_d: A single Brady violation does not establish a pattern sufficient for Canton failure-to-train liability
explanation: Connick: Canton failure-to-train requires a pattern; single Brady violations — even egregious ones — do not satisfy the pattern requirement.

stem: A state's auditor general fires three employees, allegedly in retaliation for whistleblowing. The employees sue her in personal capacity for damages under § 1983. Under Hafer v. Melo:
opt_a: The employees may sue only the state, not the individual
opt_b: The state's sovereign immunity transfers to the personal-capacity defendant
opt_c: The auditor general is absolutely immune for all decisions made in office
opt_d: The auditor general is a 'person' under § 1983 in personal capacity, even though her conduct occurred while in office
explanation: Hafer v. Melo: personal-capacity defendants are 'persons' under § 1983 regardless of when the challenged conduct occurred; SSI does not transfer.

stem: A state prisoner files a federal habeas petition without first presenting his federal claim to the state's highest court. The federal court should:
opt_a: Dismiss for failure to exhaust state remedies, with leave to refile after exhaustion
opt_b: Stay proceedings to allow exhaustion in parallel state proceedings
opt_c: Reach the merits because federal habeas is independent of state-court review
opt_d: Dismiss with prejudice because exhaustion is jurisdictional
explanation: § 2254(b) requires exhaustion of state remedies; failure to exhaust leads to dismissal without prejudice (or stay-and-abeyance in mixed-petition situations).

stem: A state court in 2010 reviewed a defendant's confrontation-clause claim and applied a rule from Crawford v. Washington that the U.S. Supreme Court announced in 2004 and has consistently applied. The state court's application was 'opposite' the result the Supreme Court reached on materially indistinguishable facts. Under § 2254(d)(1):
opt_a: The state-court decision is shielded from federal habeas review under AEDPA
opt_b: The state-court decision involved an 'unreasonable application' of clearly established federal law
opt_c: The state-court decision was 'contrary to' clearly established federal law
opt_d: The federal court must defer to the state-court interpretation of Crawford
explanation: Williams v. Taylor: 'contrary to' captures cases where the state court reaches the opposite result on materially indistinguishable facts.

stem: A state court correctly identified Strickland v. Washington as the governing rule for ineffective-assistance claims but applied it in a way that no fairminded jurist could agree with. Under § 2254(d)(1):
opt_a: The state-court decision was 'contrary to' clearly established federal law
opt_b: The state-court decision is shielded under AEDPA's deferential standard
opt_c: The federal court must remand for state-court reconsideration before granting relief
opt_d: The state-court decision involved an 'unreasonable application' of clearly established federal law
explanation: Williams v. Taylor: 'unreasonable application' captures cases where the state court chose the right rule but applied it unreasonably (Richter — no fairminded jurist could agree).

stem: A federal habeas petitioner seeks to develop new evidence in federal court showing his trial counsel was ineffective. The state court adjudicated the IATC claim on the merits. Under Cullen v. Pinholster:
opt_a: § 2254(d)(1) review is limited to the record before the state court at the time of its merits adjudication
opt_b: New evidence is admissible only if the petitioner can show diligent state-court development
opt_c: The petitioner may proceed in federal court only if § 2254(d)(2) factual review is also satisfied
opt_d: The federal habeas court must consider all relevant new evidence to ensure full and fair review
explanation: Pinholster: when § 2254(d)(1) applies, federal review is limited to the state-court record, sharply curtailing new-evidence development.

stem: A defendant fails to object at trial to allegedly coerced confession evidence; the state appellate court denies the claim under a state-procedural-default rule. The federal habeas court should:
opt_a: Defer to the state-court procedural ruling without further inquiry
opt_b: Apply the cause-and-prejudice standard to determine whether to excuse the procedural default
opt_c: Reach the merits because federal courts review federal claims de novo
opt_d: Apply AEDPA's § 2254(d)(1) standard to the procedural default itself
explanation: Wainwright v. Sykes: cause-and-prejudice (or actual-innocence gateway) is the standard for excusing procedural default.

stem: A federal habeas petitioner argues that his collateral-review counsel was ineffective for failing to raise an IATC claim. Under Coleman v. Thompson:
opt_a: Collateral-review attorney error is always 'cause' under federal due-process standards
opt_b: Collateral-review counsel's negligence triggers a Strickland-style analysis under federal habeas
opt_c: Collateral-review attorney error excuses default if the underlying claim is colorable
opt_d: Collateral-review attorney error is generally not 'cause' to excuse default, with a narrow Martinez exception
explanation: Coleman: no constitutional right to collateral-review counsel means error there isn't 'cause' (Martinez carved a narrow exception for IATC-on-IATC).

stem: In an Arizona case, the state procedural rules effectively require that ineffective-trial-counsel claims be raised first on initial-review collateral review. The petitioner's collateral-review counsel was himself ineffective. Under Martinez v. Ryan:
opt_a: Ineffective collateral-review counsel may serve as 'cause' to excuse default of the substantial IATC claim
opt_b: Ineffective collateral-review counsel is never 'cause' under Coleman
opt_c: The petitioner must first establish actual innocence to excuse default
opt_d: The petitioner's claim is automatically excused because no other forum was available
explanation: Martinez v. Ryan: where state law channels IATC claims to initial-review collateral, ineffective counsel there can be 'cause' for the IATC default.

stem: After a state conviction becomes final, the U.S. Supreme Court announces a new rule of constitutional criminal procedure regarding jury selection. Under Teague v. Lane:
opt_a: The new rule applies on collateral review because federal courts apply the law in effect at the time of decision
opt_b: The new rule applies on collateral review only to capital cases
opt_c: The new rule applies on collateral review only if the state expressly opts in
opt_d: The new rule does not apply on collateral review unless it falls within the substantive-rule or watershed-procedural exceptions
explanation: Teague: new procedural rules don't apply retroactively on collateral review except for narrow exceptions (substantive rules placing conduct beyond criminal-law reach; watershed procedural rules — almost theoretical).

stem: A state prisoner whose Fourth Amendment exclusionary-rule claim was fully litigated in state court (with a suppression hearing and appellate review) seeks federal habeas relief. Under Stone v. Powell:
opt_a: Federal habeas review proceeds de novo because 4A claims are core
opt_b: Federal habeas relief is available only if the petitioner is actually innocent
opt_c: Federal habeas review proceeds under § 2254(d)(1) deference
opt_d: Federal habeas relief is unavailable because the state provided a full and fair opportunity to litigate the 4A claim
explanation: Stone v. Powell: 4A exclusionary-rule claims are not cognizable on habeas where the state gave full and fair litigation opportunity.

stem: A petitioner files a federal habeas petition more than one year after his conviction became final, but produces credible new affidavits suggesting actual innocence. Under McQuiggin v. Perkins:
opt_a: The actual-innocence showing equitably tolls AEDPA's one-year statute of limitations
opt_b: The petition must be dismissed because AEDPA's SOL is jurisdictional
opt_c: The petitioner must first secure DNA evidence corroborating innocence
opt_d: Actual innocence excuses procedural default but not the AEDPA SOL
explanation: McQuiggin: a credible actual-innocence claim under Schlup serves as an equitable gateway through AEDPA's SOL.

stem: A federal statute provides that 'any person aggrieved by an unfair employment practice' may sue. Plaintiff alleges a violation but has not personally suffered any tangible harm. After TransUnion v. Ramirez:
opt_a: The statutory authorization confers Article III standing without further inquiry
opt_b: Standing exists if plaintiff can articulate any cognizable interest
opt_c: Standing requires both Article III injury and zone-of-interests satisfaction
opt_d: Congress's authorization is not enough; plaintiff must still show concrete harm with a common-law analog
explanation: TransUnion: Congress can elevate intangible harms to injury, but only where the harm has a close historical or common-law analog. A bare statutory violation is insufficient.

stem: An environmental NGO challenges agency rulemaking, alleging only a procedural injury — failure to conduct required NEPA analysis. Under Lujan and Summers v. Earth Island:
opt_a: Procedural injury is sufficient if the plaintiff can show good-faith reliance on the procedure
opt_b: Procedural injury supports standing if the agency's conduct was non-discretionary
opt_c: Procedural injury alone supports standing whenever Congress provides procedural rights
opt_d: Procedural injury requires a concrete underlying interest the procedure was meant to protect
explanation: Summers: a 'free-floating' procedural injury without underlying concrete interest is insufficient. Procedure must protect something concrete.

stem: A defendant's criminal conviction is reversed on appeal after he completes his sentence. He files a federal habeas petition challenging the conviction. The case is:
opt_a: Live because of collateral consequences (e.g., voting, employment) flowing from the conviction
opt_b: Moot because the sentence has been served
opt_c: Moot unless the petitioner alleges a Sixth Amendment trial right
opt_d: Live only if the petitioner is on parole or supervised release
explanation: Sibron / Spencer: collateral consequences (voting, immigration, employment) keep a habeas petition live even after release.

stem: Election-law cases challenging short-duration election regulations frequently invoke the 'capable of repetition, yet evading review' mootness exception. The exception requires:
opt_a: Short-duration challenged conduct that will likely recur to the same plaintiff
opt_b: Exceptional public importance overriding the mootness inquiry
opt_c: Express congressional designation of the dispute as recurring
opt_d: An ongoing controversy involving the same plaintiff in the same election
explanation: The two-prong test: (1) short duration that evades review; (2) reasonable expectation the plaintiff will face the conduct again.

stem: A small competitor challenges a federal subsidy granted to a dominant market incumbent, arguing the subsidy will allow predatory pricing that harms her business. Standing requires showing:
opt_a: Antitrust standing under Brunswick / Associated General Contractors
opt_b: Constitutional standing to assert market-structure harms
opt_c: Concrete competitive injury that is fairly traceable to the subsidy
opt_d: An express statutory cause of action for competitor suits
explanation: Competitor standing is well-recognized — concrete competitive harm traceable to the challenged government action satisfies Article III. Antitrust standing is a separate doctrine.

stem: A drug manufacturer files a declaratory-judgment action seeking a ruling that its product does not violate federal labeling rules, before any enforcement action has been brought. Standing exists if:
opt_a: The manufacturer can show that competitors face similar concerns
opt_b: The agency has informally indicated displeasure with the product
opt_c: The agency lacks discretion to forgo enforcement
opt_d: The manufacturer faces a credible threat of enforcement and immediate compliance burdens
explanation: Abbott Labs and SBA v. Driehaus: pre-enforcement DJ standing requires credible threat of enforcement plus concrete compliance burden or chilling effect.

stem: A consumer alleges that a credit reporting agency failed to send her a required disclosure under FCRA. She suffered no other identifiable harm. After TransUnion:
opt_a: Standing exists because Congress identified the disclosure as a consumer right
opt_b: Standing exists only if the consumer also suffered emotional distress
opt_c: The bare statutory violation is unlikely to constitute Article III injury without a concrete consequence
opt_d: Informational injuries are categorically sufficient under federal disclosure statutes
explanation: TransUnion narrowed informational-injury standing — bare statutory violations are insufficient without concrete downstream harm.

stem: An agency's enforcement of a regulation is committed to its discretion. A trade association challenges the regulation pre-enforcement. Ripeness analysis under Abbott Labs:
opt_a: Requires the agency to certify that enforcement is imminent
opt_b: Treats discretionary enforcement as inherently unripe
opt_c: Asks whether the issue is fit for review and whether withholding review imposes hardship
opt_d: Requires waiting for an actual enforcement action against an association member
explanation: Abbott Labs's two-prong test (fitness + hardship) governs pre-enforcement DJ ripeness; discretionary enforcement does not categorically defeat ripeness.

stem: Generally, a litigant cannot assert another person's rights, but the Supreme Court has recognized exceptions. Third-party (jus tertii) standing requires:
opt_a: An attorney-client relationship between the litigant and the third party
opt_b: A showing that the third party has waived the right being asserted
opt_c: Close relationship between the litigant and the third party, plus hindrance to the third party's own assertion
opt_d: Express congressional authorization to assert another's rights
explanation: Powers v. Ohio articulates the third-party standing doctrine: (1) injury to plaintiff; (2) close relationship; (3) hindrance to third party.

stem: Plaintiffs challenge a state's certification of presidential electors. After Bush v. Gore, courts have generally treated such challenges as:
opt_a: Subject to political-question and standing limits, but not categorically nonjusticiable
opt_b: Reviewable only by the U.S. Senate during electoral certification
opt_c: Reserved exclusively to state courts
opt_d: Always nonjusticiable political questions
explanation: Bush v. Gore did not declare electoral disputes categorically nonjusticiable; it applied narrow Equal Protection analysis. Subsequent cases address such disputes case-by-case.

stem: Congress passes a statute providing that 'no court of the United States shall have jurisdiction to consider any claim regarding the validity of executive immigration determinations.' The statute also withholds jurisdiction from state courts. The strongest Article III challenge:
opt_a: The statute is invalid because Congress cannot strip jurisdiction over constitutional claims
opt_b: The statute is invalid because Article III's vesting clause is mandatory
opt_c: The statute violates the Suspension Clause whenever immigration detention is involved
opt_d: The statute combined with state-court preclusion leaves federal claims with no forum, raising due-process concerns
explanation: Where state courts also lack jurisdiction, the structural concern about leaving federal rights without a forum becomes acute — particularly for constitutional claims (Story / Ratner / external due-process limits).

stem: A statute requires federal magistrate judges to issue advisory rulings on the constitutionality of administrative regulations before they take effect. The statute is most vulnerable on the ground that:
opt_a: Advisory rulings violate the Take Care Clause
opt_b: Magistrates lack authority to interpret administrative regulations
opt_c: Magistrates cannot bind the agency to constitutional interpretations
opt_d: It calls for advisory opinions outside any case or controversy, in tension with Hayburn's Case
explanation: Hayburn's Case forbids federal-judicial actors from issuing advisory opinions outside concrete cases. Magistrates inherit this constraint from Article III's case-or-controversy requirement.

stem: An executive agency adjudicates civil penalty proceedings for regulatory violations. The agency's authority to do so under Atlas Roofing rests on:
opt_a: The 7th Amendment's lack of historical guarantee for civil penalties
opt_b: Article III's flexibility for federally created rights
opt_c: The parties' implied consent to administrative adjudication
opt_d: The 'public rights' exception, given the federal regulatory origin of the enforced rights
explanation: Atlas Roofing: civil-penalty adjudication for federally created public rights is permissible in non-Article III tribunals.

stem: Bankruptcy courts may not enter final judgment on a counterclaim that is 'not necessarily resolved' by claim allowance — even when the parties have not objected. After Wellness International:
opt_a: Knowing-and-voluntary consent of the parties allows final adjudication of Stern claims
opt_b: Consent does not save Stern claims because the structural protection is non-waivable
opt_c: Stern claims may be adjudicated only by Article III judges
opt_d: Bankruptcy courts may always enter final judgment with party consent regardless of Stern
explanation: Wellness: party consent (knowing and voluntary) saves Stern claims for bankruptcy-court final adjudication.

stem: Under 28 U.S.C. § 636(b), a magistrate's report on a dispositive motion is reviewed by the district court:
opt_a: With appellate-style deference
opt_b: De novo as to objected-to portions of the proposed findings
opt_c: Under clear-error review
opt_d: Under abuse-of-discretion review
